explanation_box.text: This reptile has good claws for digging holes that are deep enough to keep their eggs protected from predators.
This reptile doesn't have claws; it is much harder for it to dig deep holes in the ground. Because its holes are shallow, predators are more likely to eat the eggs.
This reptile is red, and red reptiles produce healthier offspring than other colored reptiles.
This reptile is green, and green reptiles have offspring with fragile health.
This reptile is green, and green reptiles produce weaker offspring.
Red reptiles generally produce hardier offspring that are more likely to survive than offspring of different colors.
Green reptiles lay more eggs that aren't viable.
Green reptiles sometimes get lucky during incubation and have more viable offspring than typical of them.
Red reptiles are more likely to have healthy offspring.
Some years, red reptiles produce fewer healthy offspring than in most years.
This green reptile kicked ass this year and stayed up night and day to supervise their eggs.
This green reptile had a better year than normal.

prompt_continuous: Reptiles varied in whether they were GREEN or RED. Did being GREEN or RED make a difference to fertility? If you did not notice this feature or believe it did not make a difference to fertility, please hit no and proceed.
question_text: Did color matter to the fertility of reptiles?

prompt_continuous: Reptiles varied in whether they had ROUND feet or POINTY, CURLY feet. Did having CURLY/POINTY or ROUND feet make a difference to fertility? If you did not notice this feature or believe it did not make a difference to fertility, please hit no and proceed.
question_text: Did foot shape matter to the fertility of reptiles?
